summary: High‑energy Santa Fe show with Jerry on fire and monster jams
blurb: The band roars through a blistering first set and delivers a seamless, high‑octane second set highlighted by an epic "Other One > Goin' Down The Road > Saturday Night" sequence. Phil’s bass leads the charge while Brent’s keyboards fill every gap, making this a must‑listen for 1983 fans.
review: September 10, 1983 at Santa Fe Downs captured one of the Dead’s most electrifying afternoons. From the opening burst of "The Music Never Stopped" through a tight‑grooving "They Love Each Other," the first set never lets up, with Jerry Garcia charging his guitar on “Big Railroad Blues” and tearing into the classic "China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider" combo. Phil Lesh is front‑and‑center all night, his rumbling bass driving the band forward and even stepping to the mic for a memorable vocal moment on "Cold Rain And Snow." Brent Mydland’s keyboards weave through every jam, from the soaring “Playing In The Band” into a monster second‑set suite: "China Doll > Jam > Drums > Space > The Other One > Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad > One More Saturday Night," capped off by an unforgettable birthday drum solo from Mickey Hart that reportedly conjured a double rainbow. Across multiple recordings—soundboard, audience and unknown sources—the performance is consistently praised as "on fire" and "high‑energy," with the SBD capture (gd1983-09-10.139299.sbd.eaton.miller.flac1648) delivering crisp, clean sound that lets every nuance shine.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All eight recordings are marked positive; reviewers repeatedly use language like "on fire," "stellar," "monster Other One" and praise the entire setlist, indicating strong consensus of exceptional playing across the show.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture—crisp, clean with superb instrument separation—preserves the high‑energy performance and includes the full set without dropouts.

key_highlights: Jerry’s guitar is on fire all night, especially on "Big Railroad Blues" and the "China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider" pair.
Phil’s bass leads the band, highlighted by a vocal moment on "Cold Rain And Snow" and a rumbling presence throughout.
The epic "Other One > Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad > Saturday Night" suite delivers a monster jam that defines the second set.
Rain‑induced break adds drama; Mickey Hart’s birthday drum solo sparked a double rainbow, amplifying the show’s legend.
song_highlights: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia is described as charging, on fire and killer throughout; his solos on "Big Railroad Blues" and the "China > Rider" combo are standout highlights.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh dominates with rumbling mastery, front‑and‑center bass lines, and a memorable vocal moment on "Cold Rain And Snow," driving the band’s high‑energy flow.
band_performance.Bob: Weir provides solid rhythm work; mixed feedback notes an abrasive slide on "New Minglewood Blues" but overall contributes steady guitar foundation.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland shines with keyboard brilliance, filling every jam, extending improvisations after Jerry’s brief exit, and delivering tight support across the entire set.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart is noted as slightly rigid early but delivers a powerful birthday drum solo that became a highlight; his playing adds weight to the rhythm section.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming is praised for solid, driving work that underpins the fast‑paced, high‑energy performance.

must_listen_sequences: Beat It On Down The Line
Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
